findings.message: `.claude/rules/08-orchestration.md` now requires planner review before implementation for every task, but `.claude/skills/track-plan/SKILL.md` still explicitly skips Phase 1.5 in Focused/Quick mode. That leaves the repo with mutually exclusive instructions for existing-spec updates, so users cannot tell whether planner review is required.

`.claude/rules/10-guardrails.md` bans `2>/dev/null`, but the existing planner/research examples in `.claude/skills/track-plan/SKILL.md` still use that redirect. As written, the new guardrail makes documented commands non-compliant and will cause avoidable retries unless those examples are updated in the same change.

`.claude/rules/11-subagent-model.md` introduces `M+ difficulty` and `premium/deep-think` without defining either term or mapping them to a concrete model. The planner override rule is therefore not actionable: readers cannot tell when it applies or which model to choose.

Step 2c switches reviewer agents to the new `review-fix-lead` status contract, but Step 2d still says to collect per-agent JSON verdicts and merge findings arrays. That leaves `/track:review` with incompatible output shapes, so the orchestrator cannot reliably aggregate results or tell when a scope is actually complete.

This redirect rule is broader than the underlying bash-write-guard behavior: it bans "other stderr redirects" outright, which reads as forbidding `2>&1`/FD duplication even though the existing guard convention explicitly allows that pattern. As written, the rule conflicts with current command guidance and will block common stderr-capture usage.

This planner gate conflicts with the existing Focused/Quick exception in `.claude/skills/track-plan/SKILL.md`, which still skips Phase 1.5 for existing-spec updates. As written, the repo gives contradictory instructions on whether planner review is required for prompt-only/quick edits.

Banning `2>/dev/null` here makes several still-shipping command examples in `.claude/skills/track-plan/SKILL.md` and other guidance non-compliant. The new rule will force avoidable retries unless those examples are updated in the same change or the ban is narrowed to the actual hook failure mode.
`M+ difficulty` and `premium/deep-think` are not defined anywhere in the repo, so the planner tier rule is not actionable. Readers cannot tell when it applies or which concrete model tier to choose.

The claude-provider path still says to wait for all agents to complete before collecting statuses, but the new flow says full-model confirmation should launch immediately when a scope reports `completed`. That leaves two incompatible execution orders in the same command and breaks the stated per-scope pipelining for `claude-heavy`.

`M+ difficulty` and `premium/deep-think` are not defined anywhere in this policy set, so the planner model-tier rule is not actionable: readers cannot tell when it applies or which concrete model to pick.

When a full-model confirmation finds new issues, the relaunch path does not pass the prior findings/fixes into the next `review-fix-lead` run. That drops the structured context needed to fix the regression that triggered the rerun and can send the agent back into a fresh review loop over the same scope.

The ForgeCode integration example is wrong: ForgeCode’s MCP docs configure servers through `.mcp.json` / `forge mcp import`, not a `forge.yaml` `mcp_servers` block. As written, readers will copy a config shape ForgeCode does not document or support. Source: https://forgecode.dev/docs/mcp-integration/

The cost comparison is misleading. ForgeCode’s pricing page has Free/Pro/Max subscription tiers with included prompts and unlimited requests, so the report’s "service fee + per-token API" framing and the derived 5-10x cost estimate are unsupported unless this is explicitly scoped to a self-managed API-only setup. Source: https://forgecode.dev/pricing/

The MCP example points readers at a `forge.yaml` `mcp_servers` block, but ForgeCode’s MCP docs use `.mcp.json` and `forge mcp import` instead. This config snippet would mislead anyone trying to wire the integration. Source: https://forgecode.dev/docs/mcp-integration/

The cost section treats ForgeCode as a per-token/API-only tool and says Claude Pro/Max subscriptions are unusable, but ForgeCode’s pricing page lists Free/Pro/Max plans and the Max plan includes unlimited requests. Unless the analysis is explicitly scoped to a self-managed API-only setup, the 5-10x estimate is unsupported. Source: https://forgecode.dev/pricing/

The cost section misstates ForgeCode's pricing model: the official pricing page shows Free/Pro/Max plans with included model access, and Pro/Max do not require separate API keys. Framing ForgeCode as a per-token/API-only tool and deriving the 5-10x cost comparison from that is only valid for a self-managed API-key setup, not ForgeCode in general. Source: https://forgecode.dev/pricing/

The ForgeCode pricing/cost section is stale and internally inconsistent with the current official pricing page (https://forgecode.dev/pricing/). That page lists Free ($0), Pro ($20/month), and Max ($200/month, currently $0 for a limited time), and says Pro/Max include premium model access without requiring separate API keys. This report instead says Max is $100/month, claims ForgeCode requires a separate `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` for Claude models, and concludes there is no subscription-plan support. Please correct this before using the report for an adoption decision.

ForgeCode skills and agents are described here as YAML files, but ForgeCode’s current docs say both are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter (`.forge/skills/<skill>/SKILL.md` and `.forge/agents/*.md`). This will mislead readers about the required file layout and extension.

The MCP example says `forge mcp import .mcp.json`, but ForgeCode documents `forge mcp import` as taking a JSON string; `.mcp.json` is edited directly and then reloaded with `forge mcp reload`. The example should be corrected so users do not follow a broken setup step.

ForgeCode pricing is still mischaracterized here. The current official pricing page lists Free ($0), Pro ($20/mo with 500 prompts/month), and Max ($200/mo, currently free unlimited) and says Pro/Max include premium AI models without requiring separate API keys. This block instead describes per-token API-key billing and daily request caps, which would mislead readers making an adoption decision. Source: https://forgecode.dev/pricing/

The TermBench section states ForgeCode is '#1 at 81.8%,' but the public Terminal-Bench 2.0 leaderboard currently shows Forge Code/Gemini 3.1 Pro at 78.4% in rank 1 and Claude Code/Opus 4.6 at 58.0% in rank 33. If this is a model-specific filtered result, it needs to say so explicitly; otherwise the rank/score are wrong.

The final verdict says ForgeCode has 'No subscription plan support,' but the cost section immediately above lists Free/Pro/Max subscription tiers and pricing. This contradiction makes the cost-based recommendation unreliable.

`plan/` branch workflow ends with `/track:archive <id>` immediately after `/track:activate`, but the archive command only accepts tracks already in `done` state. Following this new sequence would tell users to archive an active track before implementation even starts.

The agent comparison table marks ForgeCode's `muse` agent as non-editing, but ForgeCode's current docs list `muse` as `read + write`. That makes the comparison materially inaccurate for readers deciding how ForgeCode's agent model differs from SoTOHE's.

The `forge-guarded` agent example is not a valid ForgeCode agent definition: current ForgeCode docs require YAML frontmatter delimited by `---` and a unique `id`, but this snippet is just bare fields inside a markdown block. Anyone copying it will get an agent file that ForgeCode cannot load.

The recommended CLI+Skill path says the existing `sotp` CLI already has the needed state-management commands, but the concrete examples use subcommands that do not exist in this repo (`sotp review check`, `sotp verify spec-signals`, `sotp track transition --id ... --task ... --to ...`). As written, the proposed 'zero-cost' integration path is not executable.

The final assessment still says ForgeCode has 'No subscription plan support' and relies on per-token API billing, which directly contradicts both the pricing section earlier in this same document and ForgeCode's current pricing page showing Free/Pro/Max subscription tiers. This leaves the report with a stale cost conclusion after the earlier pricing fix.

The agent comparison table is wrong for `muse`: ForgeCode docs describe Muse as `read + write` for planning and analysis, not read-only/no file modification. This misstates the default access level and role.
The skills row misstates ForgeCode skills format. ForgeCode's skills docs describe `.forge/skills/<skill>/SKILL.md` as a plain markdown file; YAML frontmatter applies to agents, not skills.
